F.C. TRANSMITTAL OF CORRESPONDENCE

CORRESPONDENT [handwritten: Lethe, Cornho - [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]
DATE
REC'D. 3-21

DATED 3-10 RE: Committee filing

ADMINISTRATION                CONFLICTS/LEGAL              ENFORCEMENT
__BOB T.                      __TOM S.                     __BOB B.
__LINDA                       __DWIGHT                     __ROGER
__BEV                         __SARAH                      __MARTIN
                              __ALICE                      __RON
EXECUTIVE                     __GINA T.
__TOM H.                      __PATSY                      TA&A
__TED                                                      __JOHN K.        __JAY
__CARLA                                                    ✓GINA S.K.       __RICH
__JEANNE                                                   __MARYANN        __HELEN-L.A
✓BOB S.                                                    __BERMA
                                                           __JOHN G.


_____INFORMATION        ____REQUEST FOR ADVICE--MUST BE ANSWERED WITHIN 21 DAY

[handwritten: Gina], PLEASE:

____TAKE WHATEVER ACTION YOU CONSIDER APPROPRIATE
✓REPLY DIRECT BY [handwritten: 3-2?] --COPY TO [handwritten: Patsy]
____PREPARE SUGGESTED REPLY AND RETURN TO ____________
____COORDINATE REPLY WITH ____________

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660           322-5901           322-5641         322-5444

March 26, 1980


Betty Combs
California Life Underwriters
Political Action Committee
333 Hegenberger Road, Suite 607
Oakland, CA 94621

A 80-03-018

Dear Ms. Combs:

Based on the facts outlined in your letter to the
Commission dated February 8, 1980 received by us on March
12, 1980, the pledges received by your organization in
the manner described are not pledges under the law since
there is no enforceable promise to make a payment.

Very Truly Yours,

Gina Siciliano-Kutchins
Political Reform Consultant II

FPPO
MAR 12 9 09 AH '80

10 March 1980

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

To Whom It May Concern:

The enclosed letter was mailed to the FPPC on February 8 and
as of today I have received no reply. I assume it was never
received and consequently I am asking again for a response
to my inquiry.

Since the Board of Trustees of the California Life Underwriters
Political Action Committee will be meeting on March 24 to discuss
this and other matters, I would sincerely appreciate receiving
a prompt response.

Thank you for your cooperation.

Sincerely,

Betty Combs

Betty Combs

/bc

Enclosures

CALIFORNIA
LIFE UNDERWRITERS'
POLITICAL ACTION
COMMITTEE

I.D. No. 743365

***

1979 - 1981
BOARD OF TRUSTEES

Chairman
HOWARD E. NEVONEN, CLU

DAL J. DARRACO, CLU (1)
EMIL HEIN, CLU (2)
JAMES P. ELLMAN, CLU (3)
ROY E. CODDING, III, CLU (4)
MEL R. HEBERT, CLU (5)
JOHN E. CROSSMAN, CLU (6)
LOUIS J. CASSANI, CLU (7)
GUY E. BAKER, CLU (8)

BETTY COMBS
SECRETARY & TREASURER

LINDA VANDERLINDEN
ASSISTANT TREASURER

***

CAL-LUPAC

333 Hegenberger Road
Suite 607
Oakland,
California 94621
(415) 638-2450

February 8, 1980


The Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California  95814

To Whom It May Concern:

As the Secretary and Treasurer of the California Life
Underwriters Political Action Committee it is my
responsibility to file Form 420 - Recipient Committee
Campaign Statement. Schedule D requires the reporting
of all pledges. I am uncertain as to exactly what a
pledge is deemed to be by the Fair Political Practices
Commission and would appreciate your clarification of
this point.

Although our committee does not presently solicit pledges
it is considering the use of pledge forms in the future.
For instance, an individual would solicit contributions
over the phone and subsequent to the telephone conversation
a pledge form would be mailed to the interested contributor.
The form would indicate that the person had expressed a
desire to donate a certain amount of money to our committee
and would request the individual to send the money upon
receipt of the form. This would merely be confirmation of
a verbal pledge and would in no way be enforceable. We
would not make any attempts whatsoever to force the person to
make the payment; if he or she should decide later not to contribute
that is certainly their privilege. My question is: Is this
an enforceable promise to make a payment which is your defi-
nition of a pledge? It is our opinion since no effort would be
made to force the individual to make a contribution it would
not constitute a pledge. However, we will abide by whatever
interpretation the FPPC provides.

I would appreciate receiving your response as soon as
possible and thank you for your cooperation.

Sincerely,

Betty Combs

BC:dk

                                        Very Truly Yours,


                                        Giha Siciliano-Kutchins

Since the Board of Trustees of the California Life Underwriters
Political Action Committee will be meeting on March 24 to discuss
this and other matters, I would sincerely appreciate receiving

Thank you for your cooperation.

Sincerely,

Betty Combs

Betty Combs

February 8, 1980


The Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

To Whom It May Concern:

As the Secretary and Treasurer of the California Life
Underwriters Political Action Committee it is my
responsibility to file Form 420 - Recipient Committee
Campaign Statement. Schedule D requires the reporting
of all pledges. I am uncertain as to exactly what a
pledge is deemed to be by the Fair Political Practices
Commission and would appreciate your clarification of
this point.

Although our committee does not presently solicit pledges
it is considering the use of pledge forms in the future.
For instance, an individual would solicit contributions
over the phone and subsequent to the telephone conversation
a pledge form would be mailed to the interested contributor.
The form would indicate that the person had expressed a
desire to donate a certain amount of money to our committee
and would request the individual to send the money upon the
receipt of the form. This would merely be confirmation of
a verbal pledge and would in no way be enforceable. We
would not make any attempts whatsoever to force the person to
make the payment; if he or she should decide later not to contribute
that is certainly their privilege. My question is: Is this
an enforceable promise to make payment which is your defi-
nition of a pledge? It is our opinion since no effort would be
made to force the individual to make a contribution it would
not constitute a pledge. However, we will abide by whatever
interpretation the FPPC provides.

I would appreciate receiving your response as soon as
possible and thank you for your cooperation.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Betty Combs

sections.question: Is a pledge as described—where an individual is solicited by phone and subsequently receives a pledge form to confirm, but no enforcement effort will be made—an enforceable promise to make payment which is the FPPC's definition of a pledge?
sections.conclusion: Based on the facts outlined in your letter to the Commission dated February 8, 1980 received by us on March 12, 1980, the pledges received by your organization in the manner described are not pledges under the law since there is no enforceable promise to make a payment.
sections.question_synthetic: The California Life Underwriters Political Action Committee seeks clarification on whether non-enforceable verbal pledges confirmed by mailed forms (requiring no follow-up for payment) constitute reportable pledges under Form 420 Schedule D requirements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the pledges described by Cal-LUPAC do not constitute reportable pledges under campaign finance law because they lack an enforceable promise to make payment.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes incoming request letter dated February 8, 1980, a follow-up letter dated March 10, 1980 (due to delayed response), and the FPPC's advice letter response dated March 26, 1980. All question and conclusion sections verbatim extracted from the actual FPPC response document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is a pledge as described—where an individual is solicited by phone and subsequently receives a pledge form to confirm, but no enforcement effort will be made—an enforceable promise to make payment which is the FPPC's definition of a pledge?

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts outlined in your letter to the Commission dated February 8, 1980 received by us on March 12, 1980, the pledges received by your organization in the manner described are not pledges under the law since there is no enforceable promise to make a payment.

embedding.summary: This 1980 FPPC advice letter responds to Cal-LUPAC's inquiry about whether non-enforceable verbal pledges confirmed by mailed forms constitute reportable pledges. The FPPC concluded they do not, since there is no enforceable promise to make payment.
